{"step_by_step_thinking": "A placebo is a treatment with no active therapeutic ingredients that produces a beneficial effect due to patient expectation or psychological factors. According to the MEDCORP document (Source: MEDCORP, Document [18]), examples of placebo interventions include sugar pills, saline injections, and therapeutic rituals. Sham surgery is explicitly mentioned in the context of placebo effects in clinical practice (Source: MEDCORP, Document [5]), where it is described as a sham invasive procedure used in placebo-controlled trials. This aligns with the definition of a placebo as a sham intervention that mimics a real treatment but has no active therapeutic effect. Herbal medication with no known effect (Option A) may be considered inert, but it is not specifically defined as a placebo in the documents. Physiotherapy (Option B) and cognitive behavioral therapy (Option D) are active, evidence-based treatments with proven efficacy and are not classified as placebos. Therefore, the best example of a placebo from the options provided is sham surgery, which is a well-documented placebo intervention in medical research.", "answer_choice": "C"}
